title: ACLS – Symptomatic Bradycardia and High-Degree Heart Block
description: A patient develops symptomatic bradycardia with complete heart block during a procedure. Practise the ACLS bradycardia algorithm, atropine dosing, transcutaneous pacing, and understanding the causes and classification of heart block.
learningObjectives: Apply the ACLS bradycardia algorithm (atropine → pacing → vasopressors)
Administer atropine at the correct dose and understand its limitations
Perform transcutaneous pacing correctly and manage patient discomfort
Recognise complete (third-degree) heart block on the ECG and understand its clinical significance

states.prompt: A 74-year-old male who received IV contrast for CT angiography develops sudden HR 34 bpm (regular, wide complex QRS, no relation to P waves). BP 72/44. He is diaphoretic, confused, and complaining of chest pain. This rhythm is symptomatic. Which ACLS algorithm applies?

options.label: Symptomatic bradycardia algorithm
Cardiac arrest algorithm – pulseless
Tachycardia algorithm
Watchful waiting – bradycardia may resolve spontaneously

explanations: Correct! HR 34 with hypotension, chest pain, and confusion constitutes symptomatic bradycardia. This requires the ACLS bradycardia algorithm.
The patient is haemodynamically unstable but has a pulse (BP 72/44). This is NOT cardiac arrest – do not use the arrest algorithm.
HR 34 is bradycardia, not tachycardia.
Symptomatic bradycardia with haemodynamic compromise (hypotension, chest pain, altered mental status) requires immediate intervention.

states.prompt: The monitor shows: regular P waves at 90/min, regular wide QRS at 34/min. There is NO consistent relationship between P waves and QRS complexes. What degree of heart block is this?

options.label: Third-degree (complete) heart block – atria and ventricles beat independently
Second-degree Mobitz Type II – regular PR with random dropped beats
Second-degree Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach) – progressive PR prolongation
First-degree heart block – prolonged but consistent PR interval

explanations: Correct! No consistent P-QRS relationship = complete (third-degree) AV block. Atria and ventricles are paced by independent pacemakers (sinus and junctional/ventricular escape).
Mobitz II has consistent PR intervals with sudden dropped beats. There is no PR interval relationship here.
Wenckebach has progressive PR prolongation until a beat drops. This has NO P-R relationship at all.
First-degree block has a prolonged but CONSISTENT PR interval. This ECG shows complete AV dissociation.

states.prompt: Complete heart block confirmed, patient symptomatic. What is the FIRST pharmacological treatment per the ACLS bradycardia algorithm?

options.label: Atropine 1 mg IV, may repeat every 3–5 min (max 3 mg total)
Epinephrine 1 mg IV bolus
Amiodarone 300 mg IV
Adenosine 6 mg IV rapid push

explanations: Correct! Atropine 1 mg IV is first-line for symptomatic bradycardia. Repeat every 3–5 min up to maximum 3 mg. Note: atropine is often ineffective for complete heart block (below-bundle block).
Epinephrine 2–10 mcg/min infusion is a second-line option when atropine and pacing fail — not first-line.
Amiodarone is for tachyarrhythmias, not bradycardia.
Adenosine blocks AV conduction and would WORSEN this bradycardia — absolutely contraindicated.

checklistItems.description: Correctly gives atropine 1 mg IV as first-line bradycardia treatment

states.prompt: Atropine given. HR unchanged at 34 bpm. BP 70/42. Patient deteriorating. Atropine is typically ineffective for complete (infranodal) heart block. What is the next step?

options.label: Transcutaneous pacing (TCP) immediately + analgesia/sedation for discomfort
Give a second dose of atropine 2 mg IV and wait 10 minutes
Emergency transvenous pacing as first escalation after atropine
Defibrillate at 200 J to reset the conduction system

explanations: Correct! TCP is the bridge therapy for haemodynamically unstable bradycardia refractory to atropine. TCP is painful — give analgesia/sedation (morphine/midazolam) concurrently. Arrange transvenous pacing (TVP) as definitive therapy.
Atropine will not help complete heart block (infranodal block). A second atropine dose delays definitive therapy.
TVP is definitive but requires fluoroscopic guidance and time to set up. TCP is the immediate bridge.
Defibrillation is for ventricular fibrillation/pulseless VTach, not bradycardia with a pulse.

checklistItems.description: Correctly escalates to transcutaneous pacing with analgesia

states.prompt: TCP initiated at 70/min, 60 mA. How do you confirm ELECTRICAL capture on the monitor?

options.label: A pacing spike followed by a wide QRS complex AND palpable pulse with each spike
A pacing spike followed by any QRS-like deflection (electrical capture alone)
BP normalisation to > 100 systolic is sufficient confirmation
HR displaying 70 on the monitor indicates capture

explanations: Correct! Electrical capture = pacing spike followed by wide QRS. Mechanical capture = palpable pulse with each QRS. Both must be confirmed. Rate display alone may show artifact.
Electrical capture alone is necessary but not sufficient. Mechanical capture (palpable pulse) must also be confirmed to ensure effective cardiac output.
BP improvement follows successful mechanical capture, but confirmation of capture comes from ECG + pulse assessment.
The monitor displaying '70' reflects the set rate, not actual captured beats. The ECG and pulse confirm capture.

checklistItems.description: Correctly confirms both electrical and mechanical capture during TCP

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• Symptomatic bradycardia: atropine 1 mg IV first (max 3 mg)
• Atropine often ineffective for complete (infranodal) heart block
• TCP is the bridge: pacing spike + wide QRS + palpable pulse = capture confirmed
• Adenosine is absolutely contraindicated in bradycardia (worsens AV block)
• Arrange transvenous pacing as definitive therapy after TCP stabilisation
